channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Pope Accepts Resignation of U.S. Bishop Charged With Embezzlement
item.description: Bishop Emanuel Shaleta pleaded not guilty to stealing at least $270,000 from the St. Peter Chaldean Catholic Cathedral in El Cajon, Calif.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Gregory Bull/Associated Press
item.media:description: The St. Peter Chaldean Catholic Cathedral in El Cajon, Calif., outside San Diego.

item.title: Bennie Thompson Defeats Young Challenger in Mississippi Primary
item.description: The victory by Mr. Thompson, the state’s longest-serving Black Democrat, offers a counterpoint to the broader push nationwide for younger leadership in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Bennie Thompson, the longest-serving Black Democrat in Mississippi, won his primary race for the Second Congressional District on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Administration to Restart Global Entry Program
item.description: The program for people traveling internationally, set to come back online at 5 a.m. Wednesday, had been paused amid the shutdown of the Homeland Security Department.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Jacob Langston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Homeland Security Department had said in late February that it would pause its T.S.A. PreCheck and Global Entry programs to “refocus department personnel on the majority of travelers.”

item.title: How Trump and His Advisers Miscalculated Iran’s Response to War
item.description: In the lead-up to the U.S.-Israeli attack, President Trump downplayed the risks to the energy markets as a short-term concern that should not overshadow the mission to decapitate the Iranian regime.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Benoit Tessier/Reuters
item.media:description: In response to Iranian threats, commercial shipping has come to a standstill in the Gulf, and oil prices have spiked.

item.title: Bondi Is Said to Move to Military Housing Because of Threats
item.description: The attorney general relocated from a Washington apartment to a base in the area within the past month, according to people familiar with the situation.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The immediate catalyst for moving Attorney General Pam Bondi was an increase in threats following the capture and prosecution of President Nicolás Maduro of Venezuela, according to a senior official.

item.title: Alberto Carvalho, Suspended LAUSD Chief, Denies Wrongdoing
item.description: Through his lawyers, Alberto Carvalho, who was put on leave after the F.B.I. raided his home and office, said that his actions were appropriate but that he would respect the investigative process.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alberto Carvalho was placed on paid leave last month by the Los Angeles school board after armed federal agents searched his home and his office at the district’s headquarters.

item.title: Clayton Fuller and Shawn Harris Advance to Runoff in Marjorie Taylor Greene’s Georgia District
item.description: Clayton Fuller will face Shawn Harris, a Democrat, in an April runoff. Mr. Fuller’s win over a flashier Republican showed the power of the president’s support.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset
item.category: Shawn Harris (Georgia Democrat, Ret. Brigadier General with the United States Army)
item.media:credit: Alyssa Pointer/Reuters, Matt Hamilton, via Chattanooga Times Free Press, via Associated Press

item.title: Woman Found Innocent After 22 Years in Prison Will Not Be Deported
item.description: Carmen Mejia was cleared of murder in connection with the death of a baby in her care, who was scalded by hot water in 2003 in Austin, Texas. Federal officials said she will be allowed to stay in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk and Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Mikala Compton/Austin American-Statesman, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Carmen Mejia entering the courtroom for a hearing in Austin, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: Democrats Demand Hegseth and Rubio Testify on Iran War
item.description: Senate Democratic leaders called on President Trump to dispatch the senior cabinet officials to make the case to Congress and the American public for the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro and Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State Marco Rubio at the Capitol in January.

item.title: Iranian Military Shows It Knows How to Adapt, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: Iran appears to be targeting what it views as American vulnerabilities, including air defenses meant to guard troops and assets in the region.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new billboard in Tehran on Tuesday illustrating the succession of Iran’s three supreme leaders.

item.title: Trump Tries to Sidestep Blame for Any Civilian Deaths in Iran
item.description: The president and the Pentagon have cast blame on Iran for the mounting toll. More than 1,800 people have died in the war, including many civilians.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has repeatedly suggested a strike on a girls’ school that reportedly killed 175 people was carried out by Iran or another country.

item.title: ChatGPT, Other Chatbots Approved for Official Use in the Senate
item.description: New guidelines said Senate aides could use A.I. tools for official work, including research, drafting and editing documents, and preparing briefings and talking points for lawmakers.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The United States Capitol on Tuesday. The chief information officer for the Senate sergeant-at-arms said in a one-page memo that aides could use some chatbot tools for official work.

item.title: How Long Will the Iran War Last? Trump Offers Conflicting Answers.
item.description: Now 11 days into an expanding military campaign, President Trump and his officials have given conflicting indications on how long the United States intends the war to last.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has left wide-open the possibilities for how the war with Iran could wind down and how long it will take.

item.title: Tell Us About How You Pursue Happiness
item.description: As America nears its 250th year, we want to hear from you about this particular phrase in the country’s founding document.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times

item.title: Senate Moves Toward Passing Sweeping Housing Bill, but Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: The legislation’s progress is all the more surprising because it addresses an issue that is shaping up to be the main battleground of the midterm elections: affordability. The effort could still stall.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction on a home in Simpsonville, S.C., in January.

item.title: Gov. Kay Ivey Commutes Death Sentence of Charles ‘Sonny’ Burton
item.description: It is only the second commutation for Gov. Kay Ivey, a Republican. The inmate, Charles Burton, was scheduled to die even though the gunman’s sentence had been commuted years ago.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Matt Schulz, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Death penalty opponents had flooded the governor’s office in recent days with pleas to spare Charles Burton.

item.title: Kennedy to Undergo Rotator Cuff Surgery
item.description: The secretary of health and human services, known for his Make America Healthy Again campaign, is expected to be back at work on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is undergoing rotator cuff surgery on Tuesday.

item.title: D.C. Bar Begins Disciplinary Proceedings Against Ed Martin
item.description: A new legal filing accused Mr. Martin, a senior Justice Department official, of an unethical pressure campaign against Georgetown University.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Craig Hudson For The Washington Post, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ed Martin has overseen the efforts by President Trump to use the Justice Department to punish the president’s perceived enemies.

item.title: Voting Company Smartmatic Says It’s a Target of Vindictive Prosecution by Trump
item.description: The voting technology company’s new court filing signals how far-reaching the legal fights over elections will be in 2026.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Smartmatic voting machines in Los Angeles. The company said in a court filing that charges against it were part of President Trump’s “campaign of retribution against his perceived enemies.”

item.title: San Francisco Bay Area Residents Weigh Possibility of BART Reductions
item.description: The Bay Area Rapid Transit system was once so successful, it could rely mostly on riders to sustain itself. But the pandemic dealt BART an unusually heavy blow.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight and Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Minh Connors for The New York Times
item.media:description: BART directors say that Bay Area residents can rescue the system by passing a new sales tax in November.

item.title: Democrats Sued to Find Out Whether Trump Will Send Armed Officers to Election Sites
item.description: In a lawsuit filed Tuesday, the D.N.C. sought to compel the government to say whether it plans to deploy armed federal officers in this year’s elections.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Travis Dove for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters at a polling site last week in Rockingham County, N.C.

item.title: As Iran War Spikes Gas Prices, Americans Struggle With the Rising Cost of Living
item.description: Shocked by the rapid price increases, many Americans are scrambling to figure out how to stretch their budgets to cover an essential expense.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: A gas station in Lockhart, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: Mississippi and Georgia Elections: What to Watch in Today’s Primaries
item.description: It’s Primary Day in Mississippi, where a younger Democrat is trying to oust a House veteran, and in Georgia, where Marjorie Taylor Greene’s former seat is up for grabs.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Bennie Thompson, Democrat of Mississippi, is defending his seat against Evan Turnage.

item.title: Unlike Past U.S. Conflicts, Iran Attack Is Opposed by Most Americans
item.description: While the public has historically backed military intervention initially in international conflicts, deep polarization has left the latest strikes against Iran with record-low support.
item.dc:creator: Lily Boyce and Ruth Igielnik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Judge Is Skeptical of Penn’s Argument Against Trump Demand for List of Jews
item.description: A federal judge appeared receptive to the Trump administration’s demands for information about the University of Pennsylvania’s Jewish faculty and staff as the government pursues an antisemitism investigation.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder and Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Pennsylvania campus. The school has been fighting a request that it hand over contact information for Jewish students and staff members.
